event: Rebecca at the Well Meeting Eliezer
symbols: water poured from the jar into the trough — hospitality and generosity that fulfill the divine sign, the selfless giving that identifies Rebecca as God's chosen bride for Isaac
camels drinking — the abundance of Rebecca's hospitality exceeding what was asked, a sign of her exceptional character
well in the desert — the meeting place of destiny in the patriarchal narratives, where God's providence intersects with human action
Eliezer seated and observing — the faithful servant watching for God's answer to prayer, the model of trust in divine guidance
golden desert light — the warmth of God's providential care over the unfolding of His covenant promises
description: An orientalist painting depicting the biblical meeting of Rebecca and Abraham's servant Eliezer at the well. Rebecca stands at center, a beautiful young woman in white garments with a sash, pouring water from a large clay jar into a stone trough where camels drink. Eliezer sits to the right, wrapped in robes, observing her intently. Behind them, a caravan of richly decorated camels waits, with servants visible in the background. Other women with water jars are visible at the left.
theological_description: Alexandre Cabanel's painting illustrates one of the most theologically rich narratives in Genesis: Abraham's servant Eliezer's divinely guided mission to find a wife for Isaac. Before arriving at Nahor's well, Eliezer prayed a remarkably specific prayer: 'Let the young woman to whom I shall say, Please let down your jar that I may drink, and who shall say, Drink, and I will water your camels also — let her be the one whom you have appointed for your servant Isaac' (Genesis 24:14). Rebecca's appearance and her exact fulfillment of this sign demonstrated that God was actively guiding the establishment of the covenant line. The scene at the well is a pivotal pattern in biblical narrative — Moses meets Zipporah at a well, Jacob meets Rachel at a well — these 'betrothal type-scenes' always signal God's covenantal providence in joining the right partners at the right time. Rebecca's extraordinary generosity in watering ten camels — an enormous labor — reveals not merely her physical strength but her character: she gives far more than asked, embodying the abundance of divine grace. Eliezer's faithfulness in following Abraham's instructions and trusting God's guidance models the obedience that characterizes covenant servants throughout Scripture. The scene stands at the hinge of patriarchal history: through this meeting, the line of promise from Abraham to Isaac will continue through Jacob and ultimately to Christ.

scripture_refs.reason: The full account of Eliezer's journey, prayer, and meeting with Rebecca at the well — the scene depicted in the painting
Eliezer's specific prayer asking God for a sign — the woman who offers water to him and his camels
Rebecca lowering her jar and offering to water the camels — the exact moment depicted in the painting
Isaac took Rebecca and she became his wife and he loved her — the fulfillment of the covenant purpose of this meeting
The excellent wife — Rebecca's industriousness and generosity at the well prefigure the Proverbs portrait of the ideal woman
